attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with three months of worsening fatigue, intermittent abdominal pain, and difficulty concentrating at work. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. He works as a battery recycling plant operator and has been in this role for five years. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His abdomen is soft with mild generalised tenderness. Neurological examination is normal.

Blood tests reveal:

Haemoglobin 115 g/L (130-170)
Mean cell volume 85 fL (80-100)
White cell count 7.2 x 10^9/L (4.0-11.0)
Blood lead level 0.72 µmol/L (<0.48)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management?
options: Continue work with monthly blood lead monitoring
Immediate removal from workplace and iron supplementation
Immediate removal from workplace and chelation therapy
Continue work with improved personal protective equipment
Continue work and repeat blood lead level in three months
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with symptoms consistent with chronic lead toxicity, including fatigue, abdominal pain, and cognitive symptoms. His occupational history of working in a battery recycling plant is a significant risk factor for lead exposure. The blood lead level of 0.72 µmol/L is significantly elevated above the safe threshold of 0.48 µmol/L.

Immediate removal from workplace and chelation therapy is the most appropriate management for symptomatic patients with blood lead levels above 0.48 µmol/L. Chelation therapy helps remove lead from the body, while workplace removal prevents further exposure.

Continue work with improved personal protective equipment is inadequate given the high blood lead level and presence of symptoms. Even with improved protection, ongoing exposure would be dangerous.

Continue work and repeat blood lead level in three months would allow dangerous ongoing exposure. The current level already warrants immediate intervention.

Immediate removal from workplace and iron supplementation addresses only the mild anaemia and not the underlying lead toxicity. While iron deficiency can coexist with lead poisoning, chelation is required to treat the lead toxicity.

Continue work with monthly blood lead monitoring is inappropriate as it allows continued exposure despite levels already being well above the threshold for intervention.

Lead exposure primarily occurs through two main routes: inhalation of very small lead particles in the air and ingestion of lead-containing dust or soil. While lead can contact skin, very little passes directly into the bloodstream through intact skin. However, lead on unwashed skin can be accidentally ingested. This understanding of exposure routes is crucial for prevention strategies in occupational settings. Lead particles can persist in the environment for many years after falling onto soil or water. People in high-risk occupations require proper workplace controls and regular monitoring.
learningPoints: Lead poisoning commonly presents with non-specific symptoms including abdominal pain, fatigue, and cognitive changes.
Occupational history is crucial in diagnosing heavy metal exposure.
Blood lead levels above 0.48 µmol/L require removal from exposure and consideration of chelation therapy.
Lead exposure occurs primarily through inhalation of particles or ingestion of contaminated dust/soil, with minimal absorption through intact skin.
references: Needleman H. Lead poisoning. Annu Rev Med. 2004;55:209-222.

Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. Managing elevated blood lead levels among adults. [Internet]. Atlanta, GA: CDC; Available at: https://www.cdc.gov/lead-prevention/symptoms-complications/index.html

Fact Sheet Reducing your lead exposure. Government of South Australia, SA Health. [cited 2025 Mar 11] Available at: https://www.sahealth.sa.gov.au/wps/wcm/connect/8506620049479288bf42bf0396dff19a/Fact+Sheet+-+reducing+your+lead+exposure.pdf?MOD=AJPERES&CACHEID=ROOTWORKSPACE-8506620049479288bf42bf0396dff19a-nKPJ35-
